sections.heading: Complications
sections.content: Disease-related complications in cutaneous lymphomas include pruritus, which affects nearly 90% of patients with MF/SS and significantly impairs quality of life [11]. Infections are frequent, particularly cutaneous bacterial infections (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus) and viral infections (e.g., herpes simplex virus), which can lead to bacteremia or sepsis, especially in erythrodermic patients [12]. In SPTCL, HLH is a serious complication occurring in 15%–25% of cases and is associated with high mortality [9]. LyP is associated with an increased risk of secondary lymphomas, including MF and PC-ALCL [13]. Treatment-related complications vary by therapy. Chemotherapy can cause myelosuppression, mucositis, and increased infection risk. Immunotherapy with mogamulizumab is associated with infusion-related reactions (37%), skin eruptions (25%), and diarrhea (14%), with grade 3 pyrexia (4%) and cellulitis (3%) [14]. Brentuximab vedotin, an anti-CD30 antibody-drug conjugate, commonly causes peripheral neuropathy (69% of patients), which usually resolves or improves over time [15]. Bexarotene frequently causes hypertriglyceridemia (79%), hypercholesterolemia (71%), and hypothyroidism (52%), requiring laboratory monitoring [16]. Alemtuzumab, an anti-CD52 antibody, carries a high risk of cytomegalovirus (CMV) reactivation and opportunistic infections, necessitating prophylaxis [17]. Radiation therapy (RT) can cause skin irritation, erythema, and long-term risks of secondary skin malignancies, especially with cumulative ultraviolet exposure from phototherapy [18]. Surgery is rarely discussed but may lead to wound healing complications.
